9 min read
Just now

--

For years, artificial intelligence was marketed as something almost magical.
“The cloud.” “Digital intelligence.” “Software eating the world.”
The language felt weightless, as though intelligence itself had escaped physical reality and become pure abstraction floating invisibly through fiber optic cables.
But the truth is far more industrial.
Behind every AI-generated image, chatbot response, recommendation algorithm, streaming service, and “instant” digital experience sits an enormous physical machine: data centers, substations, transmission lines, cooling towers, backup generators, industrial chillers, water systems, and vast fields of processors consuming staggering amounts of electricity.
The modern AI revolution is not merely a software revolution. It is an infrastructure revolution. And increasingly, it is an energy crisis hiding behind clean user interfaces.
The Race Toward Infinite Compute
The technology industry currently behaves as though there is no upper limit to computational demand.
Every improvement in AI capability is treated as justification for larger models, larger training runs, larger data centers, larger power contracts, and larger infrastructure footprints.
Yet an uncomfortable question now hangs over the industry:
Why are we still scaling so aggressively when today’s AI systems are already transformative for most people?
Current AI models can already write essays, summarize research, generate software, tutor students, automate customer support, produce art and video, analyze spreadsheets, translate languages, and replace significant amounts of administrative labor.
For most users, the difference between a model that is “excellent” and one that is “slightly more excellent” often provides diminishing real-world value.
But the infrastructure cost behind those marginal improvements may increase exponentially.
Society may soon spend twice the energy and resources for improvements most users barely perceive.
The Obsession With Speed
The problem extends beyond AI models themselves.
Modern digital culture has become obsessed with immediacy. A homepage must load instantly. A movie must start immediately. A chatbot must answer in under a second. Recommendations must refresh continuously. Entire engineering organizations now exist to shave milliseconds off user experiences. But very few people ask whether these tiny gains are actually worth their physical cost.
What happens if a streaming platform allows its homepage to load half a second slower? What happens if an AI assistant pauses for one second before answering simple questions? What happens if background recommendation systems update less aggressively during peak electrical demand?
Most users would barely notice. But the infrastructure implications could be enormous.
The Tyranny of Peak Demand
The issue is not average compute usage. It is peak compute usage.
If hundreds of millions of users expect instantaneous service simultaneously, companies must build massive amounts of excess infrastructure capable of handling those surges. This is similar to highways.
Cities build giant road systems not because traffic is constant all day, but because society demands immediate movement during rush hour peaks.
AI infrastructure works the same way. A system optimized for instantaneous response at all times requires more GPUs, more power plants, more cooling systems, more substations, more transmission lines, and more backup infrastructure than a system designed with even modest flexibility.
Small reductions in peak demand can dramatically reduce total infrastructure requirements.
The One-Second Sacrifice
Ironically, many forms of AI may not need to be instant at all.
Does a chatbot truly need to answer immediately when helping someone rewrite an email, brainstorm dinner ideas, summarize an article, or ask a casual question?
Would society truly suffer if some digital interactions took two seconds instead of one?
In fact, slight delays may even improve the experience psychologically.
Humans often distrust systems that answer complex questions instantly because immediate responses can feel shallow or artificial.
A short pause creates the impression of reasoning.
Future AI systems may intentionally throttle response speeds, queue low-priority tasks, reduce precision during peak demand, shift workloads geographically, or defer heavy computations until renewable energy supply is abundant.
The future of AI may look less like unlimited instant intelligence and more like intelligent load management.
AI Is Becoming Heavy Industry
One of the biggest misconceptions about artificial intelligence is that it is somehow “clean” because it is digital. In reality, AI increasingly resembles traditional heavy industry. Large data centers now consume electricity at scales comparable to small cities.
New AI campuses increasingly require dedicated substations, direct utility negotiations, industrial water access, natural gas backup generation, grid-scale battery systems, and major transmission upgrades.
Entire regions are beginning to reshape their electrical grids around AI demand. The digital economy is not escaping physical reality. It is re-industrializing it.
The Most Efficient Computer Ever Built
Yet while humanity races to construct gigawatt-scale AI infrastructure, we already possess a remarkable proof that intelligence does not necessarily require enormous energy consumption.
The human brain is incredibly efficient. A human brain operates on roughly 20 watts of power. That is similar electricity use to two total common household LED light bulbs.
And yet with those 20 watts, a person can read, reason, navigate cities, recognize faces, understand humor, compose music, solve abstract problems, and maintain consciousness continuously for decades.
Modern AI systems often require thousands or millions of watts to perform narrower versions of what human beings do with astonishing efficiency every day.
This comparison is not perfect. A human body is not just a brain, and biology carries many costs that a server does not. But the contrast is still revealing. The human brain runs on roughly the power draw of a dim light bulb, yet it can read, reason, remember, recognize faces, tell jokes, navigate cities, imagine futures, and maintain consciousness for decades.
That should make us pause.
If the human brain can do so much with so little energy, then perhaps the current path of AI development is not the final form of machine intelligence. Perhaps today’s GPU-heavy AI systems are less like mature intelligence infrastructure and more like the early steam engines of cognition: powerful, transformative, but still brutally inefficient.
The Banana Thought Experiment
Consider a simple example.
A banana contains roughly 100 calories. In energy terms, that is about 116 watt-hours.
A human brain uses roughly 20 watts. In very rough terms, the energy contained in one banana could power brain-level energy use for several hours.
During that time, a person could read dozens or even hundreds of pages, depending on the difficulty of the text and their reading speed. They could summarize what they read, compare it to earlier knowledge, discuss it with someone else, form opinions, and apply those ideas creatively.
Again, the comparison is not exact. Human metabolism is not a clean electrical circuit. The body uses energy for many things besides thought. But as a metaphor, it captures something important.
Biological intelligence is incredibly energy efficient.
A person can think all afternoon on the energy contained in a snack. Meanwhile, the AI industry is building industrial-scale computing facilities to simulate slices of that same cognitive capacity.
That does not mean AI is pointless. It means our current hardware and software stack may be wildly inefficient compared with what intelligence could eventually become.
Why Brains Are So Efficient
The brain is not just a slower version of a computer. It is organized differently.
Modern AI systems often rely on dense computation. Even relatively simple prompts can activate enormous models and require significant movement of data through memory, processors, and networking systems.
Brains work differently. They are sparse, adaptive, integrated, and event-driven.
Most neurons are not firing at full intensity all the time. Biological memory and processing are deeply intertwined. The brain does not separate storage and computation in the same rigid way digital computers do. It also uses chemical, electrical, and timing-based signals that are not simply equivalent to binary ones and zeros.
This matters because moving data is one of the biggest hidden energy costs in computing. In conventional computer architecture, information constantly travels between memory and processors. The brain avoids much of that waste by computing where information is stored.
That may be one of the reasons biology can do so much with so little.
Biological Computing and the Future of AI
This is why researchers are exploring new forms of computing that are inspired by biology.
Neuromorphic chips attempt to mimic aspects of brain-like architecture. Analog computing tries to use continuous physical signals rather than forcing every operation into digital precision. Photonic computing uses light instead of electrons for certain operations. Memristors and other emerging devices may help bring memory and computation closer together.
There are also early experiments in biological computing itself, including systems that use living neurons grown on chips. These are still experimental and nowhere near replacing modern AI infrastructure. But they point toward an important possibility: the future of computing may not look like endless warehouses of GPUs.
It may look smaller, stranger, more specialized, and much more efficient.
If AI eventually becomes more brain-like, then the data center power problem could improve dramatically. Models might need less energy to reason. Inference could happen locally on devices. Specialized chips could handle common tasks with far less waste. AI systems could become more selective about when they use expensive computation and when they use lightweight approximations.
That is the optimistic scenario.
The Less Optimistic Scenario
The less optimistic scenario is that efficiency improvements do not reduce total energy demand. They simply make AI cheaper, which causes people to use far more of it.
This is a classic rebound effect.
If every AI query becomes ten times cheaper, companies may not use the same number of queries at one-tenth the energy. They may use one hundred times as many queries. AI could be embedded into every search, every email, every spreadsheet, every camera feed, every customer service interaction, every software workflow, and every home device.
In that world, efficiency gains are real but overwhelmed by demand growth.
This is why the AI power problem is not only a hardware problem. It is also a product design problem, a business model problem, and a cultural problem.
The industry has to decide whether every possible interaction deserves AI. It has to decide whether every answer needs the largest model. It has to decide whether every user deserves instant response at all times, regardless of grid conditions, water stress, or environmental cost.
Good Enough Intelligence
The most sustainable future for AI may not come from building the largest possible models. It may come from learning when smaller systems are good enough.
A simple scheduling question does not need frontier-level intelligence. A grammar correction does not need a massive reasoning model. A basic customer support question does not need the same compute budget as scientific discovery.
AI systems should increasingly route tasks based on need. Simple tasks should go to small, efficient models. More complex tasks should go to stronger models. Nonurgent work should be delayed or batch processed. Expensive computation s
